title: 13. Audio Story Mode
description: A guided tour of the highly experimental Audio Story Mode addon: import story audio, transcribe it, plan visuals, and sync generated images to playback.

steps.title: Experimental Projector Booth
steps.body: Audio Story Mode is a highly experimental addon. It turns story audio into transcript windows, image prompts, generated visuals, playback sync, and optional streaming. Start with short clips, expect rough edges, and keep a copy of any audio you care about.

steps.title: Where the Addon Lives
steps.body: Audio Story Mode appears as its own tab in the left Workspace Tabs area. If you do not see it, open Addons, find Audio Story Mode under the visuals category, make sure it is enabled, then restart Neural Companion so the addon can mount itself properly.

steps.title: Open Audio Story Mode
steps.body: Open the Audio Story Mode tab from the same left Workspace Tabs strip. This is the addon cockpit: source audio at the top, planning controls in the middle, playback and transcript sections below.

steps.title: Set the LLM Runtime First
steps.body: Audio Story uses the active LLM Runtime chosen in System Shaping -> Host for story analysis, especially when Analysis Provider is set to Current Chat Provider. Click an LLM provider tab to inspect its settings, then click the circle inside that tab to make it active before you ask the addon to analyze a story.

steps.title: Set the Visual Reply Runtime
steps.body: Audio Story does not make the final pictures by itself. It feeds prompts into the active Visual Reply runtime in System Shaping -> Host. Click a Visual Reply provider tab to inspect its settings, and click the circle inside that tab to make it active before you generate story images.

steps.title: Import the Story Audio
steps.body: Use Import Audio to choose an audiobook, narration, drama clip, or other story-shaped audio file. Shorter clips are easier while you learn because long files make transcription, planning, and image generation slower to inspect.

steps.title: Choose Playback Source
steps.body: Playback decides what you hear when the visual story runs. Play Imported Audio is the safer first pass because it keeps the original track. Use TTS Narration only when you specifically want the transcript re-spoken as a generated narration track.

steps.title: Pick a Cost Profile
steps.body: Quality profile controls how much planning and image generation the addon performs. Economy is best for a first test. Balanced is the normal starting point. Detailed and Cinematic can produce richer visuals, but they need more analysis time and image-generation budget.

steps.title: Save Reusable Settings
steps.body: Settings Preset lets you save and reload an Audio Story setup. Use it after you find a combination that behaves. Do not turn your first experimental run into a sacred artifact; let it earn the Save button.

steps.title: Set Transcription Granularity
steps.body: Transcription Granularity controls how audio is chunked for local Whisper transcription. Smaller chunks can be easier to align and inspect; larger chunks can be calmer for long passages. The Start and End second boxes let you test only part of a file before committing the whole opera.

steps.title: Plan Image Timing
steps.body: New image timing and Timing mode decide when new visuals should appear. Fixed Seconds is predictable. Scene Changes tries to group transcript chunks by story beats. If the scene detector changes images too often, use Fixed Seconds or adjust the detail settings.

steps.title: Control Continuity
steps.body: Continuity tells the visual planner how strongly to preserve characters, locations, props, and style between images. Higher continuity helps recurring faces and places stay familiar; lower continuity gives the image provider more creative room.

steps.title: Generate Ahead Carefully
steps.body: Generate Ahead can prepare upcoming visuals before playback reaches them. That helps smooth playback, but it also means wrong settings can produce several wrong images in a neat little parade. Keep it low until the transcript and style are sane.

steps.title: Set the Visual Style
steps.body: Story Image Style controls the visual direction of the generated images. Style layers can turn the same transcript into a comic book, pulp horror, retro anime, watercolor diary, romance cover, noir scene, or any other consistent style you describe.

steps.title: Change Style Live
steps.body: Apply style edits while working lets style changes affect the current story immediately. Leave it off when you want stable, repeatable visuals. Turn it on when you are actively experimenting with the visual direction.

steps.title: Show the Pictures
steps.body: After images exist, open Visual Reply to inspect the current picture, captions, and prompt text. Use this to judge whether the style prompt improved the scene and whether the chosen image provider is matching the story.

steps.title: Use a Master Prompt
steps.body: Visuals Master Prompt From Story builds a broad visual anchor from the transcript. It can improve consistency across the whole story, especially recurring characters and places. Stronger modes anchor harder, but they also make prompts longer and less nimble.

steps.title: Choose Story Analysis Depth
steps.body: Story Analysis decides how much planning happens before images are generated. Scene Only is the lighter route. Story Bible tries to preserve character, location, prop, and style memory across scenes. It can be better, slower, and occasionally very sure about something it misunderstood.

steps.title: Pick Analysis Provider and Model
steps.body: Analysis Provider controls where transcript analysis and prompt planning run. Current Chat Provider uses the active LLM Runtime selected in System Shaping -> Host. DeepSeek and Local LM Studio are explicit routes. Analysis Model can be left on Auto unless you know the exact model id you want to volunteer for story duty.

steps.title: Check Image Provider Settings
steps.body: Final image generation follows the chosen Visual Reply runtime. Runware can be useful for lower-cost test images, depending on model and size. xAI/Grok and other providers may have different quality, speed, and cost tradeoffs. Try different providers and models until the story looks right.

steps.title: Transcribe Audio
steps.body: Click Transcribe Audio after importing a file and choosing the range you want. This uses the local Whisper path already in the repo. Watch the progress bar and give it time; yelling encouragement at the progress bar remains optional and scientifically unproven.

steps.title: Read the Transcript Windows
steps.body: Transcript Windows show what the addon thinks it heard and how it split the story. Inspect this before blaming visual generation. If a name, place, or scene is wrong here, the images will faithfully sprint in the wrong direction.

steps.title: Use Scene Overrides
steps.body: Scene Overrides let you pin character cues, pin locations, force a fresh scene, force continuation, edit the current scene anchor, and add a negative prompt. This is the steering wheel for when automatic planning gets stylishly wrong.

steps.title: Play the Story
steps.body: Playback and Visual Stream is where the story actually runs. Play starts the selected audio path and advances visuals against the timeline. Pause and Stop do exactly what they say, which is a relief in a tab this experimental.

steps.title: Scrub the Timeline
steps.body: The position slider lets you move through the story after media is ready. Use it for spot checks: does this moment show roughly the right scene, mood, and cast? If not, adjust timing, continuity, or scene overrides before generating a larger set of images.

steps.title: Stream Visuals
steps.body: Stream Visuals starts a local visual stream on the chosen port. Use the URL for another screen or browser when you want the visuals away from the control panel. Keep the port ordinary, local, and boring unless you enjoy debugging network mysteries.

steps.title: Cast Only After It Works Locally
steps.body: The Cast controls can discover Chromecast-style devices and send the current visual stream there. Get local playback and streaming stable first. Casting a broken pipeline just makes the broken part larger and more theatrical.

steps.title: A Practical First Run
steps.body: For the first real test: import a short clip, use Play Imported Audio, choose Economy or Balanced, keep Generate Ahead low, use Scene Only, transcribe a small range, inspect the transcript, then play. Once that works, raise quality one knob at a time. The addon may be experimental, but your debugging does not have to be.
